content.text: Data Fetching in Effects and Race Conditions

Data fetching is the most common side effect in React applications. Fetching user profiles, posts, or search results usually depends on some piece of state — a user ID, a search query, a page number — and re-fetching when that value changes is exactly what
with a dependency array is designed for. But the asynchronous nature of network requests introduces a subtle and hard-to-spot bug: the

attrs.alt: Race condition: userId=2 resolves before userId=1 but is then overwritten

content.text: The minimal data-fetching pattern is straightforward: put a
, add the relevant dependency, and call
This pattern works correctly in the simplest case where the
changes infrequently and the server always responds in the same order requests were made. In practice, neither assumption holds.

A race condition occurs when two fetch requests are in-flight at the same time and the second response arrives before the first one. The component issues request A, the user changes the ID before A resolves, React issues request B, B resolves first and sets the correct data, then A resolves and overwrites the correct data with stale data.
The UI now shows data for ID 1 even though the user selected ID 2. This can happen on any network slower than the time between two renders — which is almost every real user's network.
The modern, idiomatic fix is to cancel the in-flight request in the cleanup function using the Fetch API's built-in
. When the component unmounts or a dependency changes, the cleanup fires, calls
, and the browser discards the response before it reaches your
as part of the request options. When
is called, the browser raises an
block checks for this specific error and silently ignores it — only genuine network failures are rethrown.
using that signal immediately rejects with an
. The response body is not read, network resources are freed, and the
was widely supported, the standard workaround was an
: a boolean variable in the effect closure that is set to
in the cleanup, and checked before calling

cancel the network request — it still completes and consumes bandwidth. But it prevents the stale response from updating React state. The AbortController approach is preferred because it also frees server resources and bandwidth.
A complete data-fetching effect should track loading and error states. Without these, the UI shows stale data during the transition between requests and has no way to display error messages.

When an effect issues multiple sequential fetches (fetch A, use the result to fetch B), each request needs its own abort handling. The cleanest approach is to abort the controller on cleanup, which aborts all in-flight requests made with that signal.
The effect callback is NOT marked async. Instead, an inner
is defined and immediately called. This keeps the cleanup return value synchronous.
When the same loading / error / abort pattern appears across many components, extract it into a custom hook. This centralises the race-condition fix and keeps component code clean.
to fetch; the hook owns the race-condition safety, loading states, and cleanup.
Why the Callback Cannot Be async
A common mistake is marking the effect callback itself as
. React expects the callback to return either nothing or a cleanup function. An async function always returns a Promise, not a cleanup function, which breaks cleanup and generates a React warning.
For production applications, consider using a data-fetching library instead of raw
. These libraries handle race conditions, caching, background refresh, and error retries out of the box.
Each library wraps the same fundamentals — fetch, abort, cache — but provides a higher-level API. Use raw
for one-off fetches or when adding a library is not justified.

In development with Strict Mode, React intentionally mounts, unmounts, and remounts every component. A correctly written effect will fire, clean up (abort the request), and fire again. On the second mount, a fresh request is made. If you see your API called twice in development, this is expected and a sign your cleanup is working correctly.

Search inputs illustrate a second problem: fetching on every keystroke floods the server and creates many race conditions simultaneously. Debouncing delays the fetch until the user stops typing, reducing the number of requests and the surface area for races.

The timeout ID is stored in a variable (not state) so it can be cancelled in the cleanup without causing a re-render. Combining debouncing with AbortController means the final request is also cancellable if the input changes again before it resolves.

Fetching a list whose source can change (user ID, filter, sort order) while pagination state is also changing produces compounded race conditions: the page number and the filter are both dependencies, and either can change independently.
changes is important: without it, the effect runs with a stale
number immediately after the query changes, fetching the wrong page of the new results.
Effects are appropriate for reads (GET requests). Mutations — POST, PUT, DELETE — are usually triggered by user events, not by state changes, so they belong in event handlers rather than effects.
An event handler can still use
if the user can navigate away before the mutation completes, but cleanup is managed manually (e.g., by attaching it to a component-level ref) rather than automatically by an effect.
Several patterns look correct but produce subtle bugs:
Stale state after navigation or fast re-renders
Effect uses stale value from a previous render
Add all referenced values to dep array
Fetching on every render (no dep array)
Network spam, infinite loop if also setState
Not resetting loading on dep change
Previous data shown briefly before new load
is correct in concept but requires explicit race-condition handling. Every fetch effect that depends on changing state needs a cleanup function that cancels or ignores the in-flight request.
two fetches in-flight; the slower one overwrites the fresher result if not cancelled.
pass signal to fetch, call abort() in cleanup — the browser discards the stale response.
a boolean set in cleanup prevents stale setState calls, but does not free network resources.
never mark the effect callback itself async — return cleanup synchronously.
reset loading=true and error=null at the start of each effect run.
useFetch consolidates the pattern and keeps components free of boilerplate.
SWR and React Query handle race conditions automatically and add caching on top.
